revision.text:
[[de:Baktiu]][[pt:Baktiu]] {{Infobox Hieroglyph |NAME = G29-V31:X1*X1-N35-N14 |NAME2 = D58-G1-V31:X1-M17-G43 N14 |NAME2-ERKLÄRUNG = Baktiu-(sebau)
''B3k.tjw-(sb3w)''
''Working (stars)'' |BILD1 = Southern cross2.jpg |BILD1-BREITE = 200px |BILD1-BESCHREIBUNG = Dekan Temat-heret-cheret }} The '''Baktiu''', or "working ({{ill|Star|en|Star|qid=Q523|lt=stars|1=Star}})" (also called '''Dekan stars'''), embody the {{ill|Egyptian mythology|en|Egyptian mythology|qid=Q205740|lt=Ancient Egyptian mythology|1=Egyptian mythology}} and {{ill|Egyptian astronomy|en|Egyptian astronomy|qid=Q751843|lt=Ancient Egyptian astronomy|1=Egyptian astronomy}} concept of {{ill|Decan|en|Decan|qid=Q5248738|lt=Decan|1=Decan}}. A specific number of stars, which could vary in quantity, formed the decan {{ill|Constellation|en|Constellation|qid=Q8928|lt=constellation|1=Constellation}}, collectively referred to as the "Dekan-Baktiu." == Astronomical Life of a Decan Star == The astronomical life of a decan star occurred in six phases: birth – ascent – performing work – descent – death – regeneration/renewal. Each of the 36 decan stars changed their status to "Dekan-Chatiu" after entering the {{ill|Duat|en|Duat|qid=Q871389|lt=Duat|1=Duat}}. In the {{ill|Book of Nut|en|Book of Nut|qid=Q2005559|lt=Book of Nut|1=Book of Nut}}, the life of a decan is described: {{Quote|He (the decan) is born ({{ill|Season of the Harvest|en|Season of the Harvest|qid=Q1248622|lt=Schemu|1=Season of the Harvest}} 16th Shemu II), which means that he rises in the sky from the Duat…Count from the day of his setting ({{ill|Season of the Emergence|en|Season of the Emergence|qid=Q2037143|lt=Peret|1=Season of the Emergence}} 6th Peret IV) to the day of his rising; that makes 70 days. These are the days he spends in the Duat […] He remains (after his rising) 80 days in the east. He spends 120 days performing work in the middle of the sky. He is present for 10 days in them ({{ill|Culmination|en|Culmination|qid=Q611114|lt=Culmination|1=Culmination}} on the 6th Peret I) […] He spends 90 days in the west. The stars spend their time in the west (therefore) because they have performed their work.|Dekan {{ill|Temat-heret-cheret|de|Temat-heret-cheret|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Temat-heret-cheret|qid=Q2402471|1=Temat-heret-cheret}} (Nutbuch, PC1, § 44b–c)|qq=}} == Dating Sirius (System of the Book of Nut) == {| class="wikitable" style="width: 98%; border: 2px solid gray;" |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight|qq=}} |colspan="7" align=center style="background:#FFE39B"|'''Dating Sirius in Memphis ({{ill|Gregorian calendar|en|Gregorian calendar|qid=Q12138|lt=Gregorian calendar|1=Gregorian calendar}})''' |- {{Ägyptologie TblHighlight|qq=}} !Year !{{ill|Heliacal rising|en|Heliacal rising|qid=Q368373|lt=Heliacal|1=Heliacal rising}}
RisingThe altitude values refer to the end of the respective night hour. Heliacal rising occurs before sunrise, acronychal setting, and acronychal culmination after sunset. !Beginning of Work
(Middle of the Sky) !Arrival
(Western Sky) !{{ill|Acronychic|lt=Acronychic|de|Akronychisch|1=Acronychic|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|13=User:Immanuelle/Acronychic|qid=Q4059812}}
Setting |- style="background:#F5F5F5" | style="text-align:center"| 2089 BCE | style="text-align:center"| {{ill|Season of the Emergence|en|Season of the Emergence|qid=Q2037143|lt=Peret|1=Season of the Emergence}} 22nd Peret II
June 30
4:10 AM Rising
Horizon altitude 2° | style="text-align:center"| {{ill|Season of the Harvest|en|Season of the Harvest|qid=Q1248622|lt=Schemu|1=Season of the Harvest}} 22nd Shemu I
September 18
4:10 AM Culmination
Horizon altitude 40° | style="text-align:center"| {{ill|Season of the Inundation|en|Season of the Inundation|qid=Q770568|lt=Achet|1=Season of the Inundation}} 22nd Achet I
January 31
6:17 PM First visible
Horizon altitude 39° | style="text-align:center"| 12th {{ill|Season of the Inundation|en|Season of the Inundation|qid=Q770568|lt=Achet|1=Season of the Inundation}} IV
April 21
6:49 PM Setting
Horizon altitude 2° |- |} == References == == Literature == * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|en|Joachim Friedrich Quack|qid=Q1396207|lt=Joachim Friedrich Quack|1=Joachim Friedrich Quack}}: ''Decans and Limb Deification: Ancient Egyptian Traditions in the Apocryphon of John.'' In: ''{{ill|Jahrbuch für Antike und Christentum|lt=Jahrbuch für Antike und Christentum|de|Jahrbuch für Antike und Christentum|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jahrbuch für Antike und Christentum|qid=Q1678614|1=Jahrbuch fuer Antike und Christentum}}.'' Volume 38, {{ill|Aschendorff Verlag|de|Aschendorff Verlag|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Aschendorff Verlag|qid=Q722410|1=Aschendorff Verlag}}, Münster 1995, pp. 97–122 ([http://archiv.ub.uni-heidelberg.de/propylaeumdok/volltexte/2009/292/ Online]). * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|fr|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Alexandra von Lieven|qid=Q2643704|1=Alexandra von Lieven}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The So-Called Book of Nut.'' The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (et al.), Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. {{translated page|de|Baktiu|version=|comment=Imported full de history, revisions duplicated and version not here due to an error with my bot related to the German being under the same pagename as the english}} [[ja:バクティウ]] [[de:Baktiu]] [[pt:Baktiu]] {{wikidata link|Q804527}}
